edges.description: Ligand-independent PDGFRB signaling transforms fibroblasts and drives the multifocal myofibromatosis tumor phenotype.
The PDGFRB-driven myofibromatosis phenotype is a benign fibrous soft-tissue tumor phenotype.
Multifocal myofibroma growth manifests as nodular lesions, including subcutaneous tissue involvement.
Myofibroma involvement of bone can present as osteolytic lesions.
Generalized infantile myofibromatosis can include cardiac myofibroma lesions.

nodes.description: Cardiac involvement by myofibromas occurs in the generalized form and is a major contributor to the poor prognosis of visceral disease.

evidence.reference_title: Diverse presentation and tailored treatment of infantile myofibromatosis: A single-center experience.
evidence.snippet: Visceral involvement included central nervous system, cardiac, gastrointestinal, muscle, bone, and subcutaneous tissue lesions.

nodes.description: Gain-of-function mutations in PDGFRB lead to ligand-independent receptor activation, promoting myofibroblast proliferation and tumor formation. The mechanism is reminiscent of multifocal venous malformations induced by TIE2 mutations.

evidence.snippet: We showed that these mutations activated receptor signaling in the absence of ligand and transformed fibroblasts.
the three other mutants were constitutively active and able to transform NIH3T3 and Ba/F3 cells to different extents.

nodes.description: Familial infantile myofibromatosis presents with benign fibrous soft-tissue tumors.

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent PDGFRB mutation causes familial infantile myofibromatosis.
evidence.snippet:
Infantile myofibromatosis (IM) is the most common benign fibrous tumor of soft
tissues affecting young children.
Infantile myofibromatosis is one of the most prevalent soft tissue tumors of infancy and childhood.

nodes.description: Myofibromas involving bone present radiographically as osteolytic lesions, often with a sclerotic rim.

evidence.reference_title: A rare case of infantile myofibromatosis and review of literature.
evidence.snippet: examination presented a pretibial soft-tissue tumor mass with calcifications and two osteolytic lesions with a sclerotic rim.
Mutations in the coding sequence of PDGFRB were identified in 6 out of 8 patients with the sporadic multicentric form of the disease and in 1 out of 8 patients with isolated myofibroma.
Gain-of-function PDGFRB mutations were found in samples from 25 children, with no mutation found in samples from adults.
Multifocal nodules with visceral lesions are associated with a poor prognosis.
